question: Why does the first angel call for worship of the Creator using the specific formula 'him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters'? Is this a direct quotation of Exodus 20:11? Verbal comparison through LXX — analyze poieo/asah bridge. Compare with other creation-worship texts (Neh 9:6, Acts 4:24, Acts 14:15, Psa 146:6). In a passage concluding with 'the commandments of God' (Rev 14:12), how does the creation formula link to the Sabbath commandment? Address the objection that this is generic creation theology. Creation-to-new-creation bookend (Gen 2:2-3 to Isa 66:22-23). Hebrews 4:9 sabbatismos.

topics.name: WORSHIP
topics.subtopic: TO BE RENDERED ONLY TO GOD
topics.entry_text: To be rendered only to God EXO 20:3; DEU 5:7; 6:13; MAT 4:10; LUK 4:8; ACT 10:26; 14:15; COL 2:18; REV 19:10; 22:8.

topics.name: CREATION
topics.entry_text: Beginning of GEN 1:1. History of GEN 1; 2. See GOD, CREATOR. See JESUS, CREATOR.

topics.name: COVENANT
topics.entry_text: Of the sabbath EXO 31:16. Of the Ten Commandments EXO 34:28; DEU 5:2,3; 9:9.

topics.name: MEMORIAL
topics.entry_text: The Passover EXO 12:14. See PASSOVER. Firstborn set apart as a EXO 13:12-16. The pot of manna EXO 16:32-34. The Feast of Tabernacles LEV 23:43. Shoulder stones of the ephod EXO 28:12. The Lord's Supper LUK 22:19; 1CO 11:24-26.

topics.name: SEAL
topics.entry_text: FIGURATIVE. Of secrecy DAN 12:9; REV 5:1. Of certainty of divine approval JHN 6:27; ROM 15:28; 2CO 1:22; EPH 1:13; 4:30; REV 7:3,4.

topics.name: SIGN
topics.entry_text: A miracle to confirm faith MAT 12:38; 16:4; 24:3,30; MRK 8:11,12; 13:4; JHN 2:11; 3:2; 4:48.

topics.name: EARTH
topics.entry_text: Created by God. See references in Psalms, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Acts establishing God as maker of heaven and earth.

verses.heading: The First Angel's Message — Creation Formula and the Call to Worship
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: The first of three angels delivers the everlasting gospel. The three imperatives (fear, give glory, worship) form the message content. The creation formula 'him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters' echoes Exo 20:11 through the LXX. This verse uses poieo (G4160), not ktizo (G2936).
verses.notes: The dative articular aorist active participle 'to poiesanti' (V-AAP-DSM) identifies the One worshipped by His creative act. The four objects (ton ouranon, ten gen, thalassan, pegas hydaton) share three of four elements with Exo 20:11 LXX. The fourth element diverges: 'pegas hydaton' replaces 'panta ta en autois.' All three imperatives are aorist — decisive, punctiliar commands. The legon participle (V-PAP-NSM) connecting v.7 to v.6 makes the worship command the CONTENT of the everlasting gospel.

verses.heading: The Fourth Commandment — Creation Rationale for the Sabbath
verses.kjv_text: Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days shalt thou labour, and do all thy work: But the seventh day [is] the sabbath of the LORD thy God: [in it] thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter, thy manservant, nor thy maidservant, nor thy cattle, nor thy stranger that [is] within thy gates: For [in] six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them [is], and rested the seventh day: wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it.
verses.context: The fourth of the Ten Commandments. The creation rationale (v.11) grounds Sabbath observance in God's creative act. The Hebrew uses asah (H6213) — 'the LORD made (asah) heaven and earth.' The LXX renders asah as poieo (epoiese), the same verb root Rev 14:7 uses (poiesanti). This is the asah/poieo bridge that links the two texts.
verses.notes: Exo 20:11 LXX: epoiese (aorist active indicative of poieo) Kyrios ton ouranon kai ten gen kai ten thalassan kai panta ta en autois. Compare Rev 14:7: poiesanti (aorist active participle of poieo) ton ouranon kai ten gen kai thalassan kai pegas hydaton. Four shared terms in sequence: poieo, ouranon, gen, thalassan. The verb choice is the decisive datum — the LXX renders H6213 asah as G4160 poieo, and Rev 14:7 picks up the LXX verb, not bara/ktizo.

verses.heading: Creation Formula Parallel — 'Made Heaven, Earth, Sea, and All Therein'
verses.kjv_text: Happy [is he] that [hath] the God of Jacob for his help, whose hope [is] in the LORD his God: Which made heaven, and earth, the sea, and all that therein [is]: which keepeth truth for ever:
verses.context: A creation-worship psalm using the exact formula of Exo 20:11. The LXX uses poiesanta ton ouranon kai ten gen ten thalassan kai panta ta en autois — concluding with 'panta ta en autois' (all that is in them), not 'pegas hydaton.' This confirms that the standard creation formula includes 'all in them,' making Rev 14:7's substitution conspicuous.
verses.notes: One of three parallel texts (with Acts 4:24 and Acts 14:15) that faithfully reproduce the Exo 20:11 formula including 'panta ta en autois.' Rev 14:7 alone departs from this pattern.

verses.heading: Creation Formula in Early Church Prayer — Follows Exo 20:11 Exactly
verses.kjv_text: And when they heard that, they lifted up their voice to God with one accord, and said, Lord, thou [art] God, which hast made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and all that in them is:
verses.context: The early church prays to the Creator using the Exo 20:11 formula. The Greek follows the LXX exactly: ton poiesanta ton ouranon kai ten gen kai ten thalassan kai panta ta en autois. This is the most exact NT reproduction of the Sabbath commandment's creation rationale.
verses.notes: Confirms the standard NT creation formula uses 'panta ta en autois.' Acts 4:24 and 14:15 both use this completion phrase; Rev 14:7 alone substitutes 'pegas hydaton.'

verses.heading: Paul and Barnabas at Lystra — Same Creation Formula
verses.kjv_text: And saying, Sirs, why do ye these things? We also are men of like passions with you, and preach unto you that ye should turn from these vanities unto the living God, which made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and all things that are therein:
verses.context: Paul redirects worship from himself to the living God who made heaven, earth, the sea, and all things therein. Uses the same Exo 20:11 creation formula as Acts 4:24.
verses.notes: Third parallel text confirming the standard formula. The pattern is consistent: Psa 146:6, Acts 4:24, Acts 14:15 all use 'panta ta en autois.' Rev 14:7's departure is deliberate.

verses.heading: The Most Expansive Creation-Worship Formula
verses.kjv_text: Thou, [even] thou, [art] LORD alone; thou hast made heaven, the heaven of heavens, with all their host, the earth, and all [things] that [are] therein, the seas, and all that [is] therein, and thou preservest them all; and the host of heaven worshippeth thee.
verses.context: Levitical prayer expanding the creation formula to include the heaven of heavens and their host. The most detailed version of the formula in the OT, connecting creation to worship explicitly.
verses.notes: The most expansive OT version of the creation formula. Uses asah (made) with heaven, earth, seas, and all therein. Adds 'thou preservest them all' — sustaining power alongside creative power.

verses.heading: Paul at Athens — Closest NT Parallel to Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: God that made the world and all things therein, seeing that he is Lord of heaven and earth, dwelleth not in temples made with hands; Neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing, seeing he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things; And hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth, and hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation; That they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after him, and find him, though he be not far from every one of us: For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring. Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art and man's device. And the times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent: Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by [that] man whom he hath ordained; [whereof] he hath given assurance unto all [men], in that he hath raised him from the dead.
verses.context: Paul at the Areopagus combines Creator God (v.24), universal scope (v.26,30), and appointed judgment day (v.31) — the exact triad of the first angel's message. Uses poieo (G4160) for God's creative act (v.24). The closest NT parallel to Rev 14:7's combination of creation, worship, and judgment.
verses.notes: Both Acts 17:24 and Rev 14:7 use poieo for God's creative act and identify God as Lord of heaven and earth. The judgment announcement (v.31: 'appointed a day to judge') parallels Rev 14:7 ('the hour of his judgment is come'). This is the NT sermon most structurally parallel to the first angel's message.

verses.heading: The Creation Sabbath — God Rested, Blessed, and Sanctified the Seventh Day
verses.kjv_text: Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them. And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made. And God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it: because that in it he had rested from all his work which God created and made.
verses.context: The institution of the Sabbath at creation, before sin, before Israel, before the ceremonial law. Three divine acts on the seventh day: shabath (ceased/rested, H7673 Qal.Wayq.3ms), barak (blessed, Piel.Wayq.3ms), and qadash (sanctified, Piel.Wayq.3ms). Gen 2:3 uniquely pairs bara (create) and asah (make) in one verse.
verses.notes: The Hebrew parsing confirms: wayyishbot (Qal wayyiqtol of shabath) = 'and he ceased'; wayebarek (Piel wayyiqtol of barak) = 'and he blessed'; wayeqaddesh (Piel wayyiqtol of qadash) = 'and he sanctified.' The three-fold action sequence establishes the seventh day's unique status at creation itself.

verses.heading: Sabbath as Owth (Sign) — Perpetual Covenant
verses.kjv_text: Speak thou also unto the children of Israel, saying, Verily my sabbaths ye shall keep: for it [is] a sign between me and you throughout your generations; that [ye] may know that I [am] the LORD that doth sanctify you. Ye shall keep the sabbath therefore; for it [is] holy unto you: every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death: for whosoever doeth [any] work therein, that soul shall be cut off from among his people. Six days may work be done; but in the seventh [is] the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD: whosoever doeth [any] work in the sabbath day, he shall surely be put to death. Wherefore the children of Israel shall keep the sabbath, to observe the sabbath throughout their generations, [for] a perpetual covenant. It [is] a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever: for [in] six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed.
verses.context: The Sabbath explicitly designated as owth (sign, H226) in v.13 and v.17 — the only commandment to receive this designation. The reason given in v.17 echoes the creation rationale of Exo 20:11: 'for in six days the LORD made (asah) heaven and earth.' Called a 'perpetual covenant' (berith olam) in v.16.
verses.notes: Hebrew parsing of Exo 31:17 confirms: owth (sign, Noun.s.Abs) + ki clause (because) + asah (Verb.Qal.Perf.3ms = made) YHWH + et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'arets (the heavens and the earth). The creation rationale is identical to Exo 20:11. The Sabbath is the ONLY commandment explicitly called owth in the OT.

verses.heading: Alternative Sabbath Rationale — Deliverance, Not Creation
verses.kjv_text: Keep the sabbath day to sanctify it, as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee. Six days thou shalt labour, and do all thy work: But the seventh day [is] the sabbath of the LORD thy God: [in it] thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter, nor thy manservant, nor thy maidservant, nor thine ox, nor thine ass, nor any of thy cattle, nor thy stranger that [is] within thy gates; that thy manservant and thy maidservant may rest as well as thou. And remember that thou wast a servant in the land of Egypt, and [that] the LORD thy God brought thee out thence through a mighty hand and by a stretched out arm: therefore the LORD thy God commanded thee to keep the sabbath day.
verses.context: Deuteronomy's version of the fourth commandment replaces the creation rationale with a deliverance rationale. The Sabbath here commemorates the Exodus. Rev 14:7 echoes the creation rationale (Exo 20:11), not the deliverance rationale (Deut 5:15), which is significant for identifying the specific allusion.
verses.notes: The use of zakar (remember, H2142) in both Exo 20:8 and Deut 5:15 frames the Sabbath as a memorial institution pointing backward, not a shadow pointing forward.

verses.heading: The Ark of the Covenant Revealed — Activating the Commandment Theme
verses.kjv_text: And the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament: and there were lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail.
verses.context: The heavenly temple opens, revealing the ark of the covenant (containing the Ten Commandments). This activates the commandment theme that runs through Rev 12:17 to 14:12, establishing the Decalogue as the backdrop for the three angels' messages.
verses.notes: The covenant-law thread: diatheke (11:19) → entole (12:17) → entole (14:12). The ark revelation at 11:19 begins the cosmic conflict unit; the commandments mentioned at 12:17 and 14:12 reference the law contained in that ark.

verses.heading: The Remnant Keeps the Commandments of God
verses.kjv_text: And the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed, which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ.
verses.context: The remnant is identified by two characteristics: keeping the commandments of God (entolas tou theou) and having the testimony of Jesus. This is the first occurrence of 'commandments of God' after the ark revelation at 11:19.
verses.notes: Greek parsing confirms: ton terounton (V-PAP-GPM, present active participle genitive plural of tereo = the ones keeping/guarding) tas entolas (N-APF, accusative plural of entole = the commandments) tou theou (N-GSM, genitive singular of theos = of God). The present participle indicates ongoing, continuous keeping. The parallel with Rev 14:12 is structural: both use tereo + entolas + tou theou.

verses.heading: The Inclusio — Commandments of God and Faith of Jesus
verses.kjv_text: Here is the patience of the saints: here [are] they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.
verses.context: The concluding verse of the three angels' messages. Forms an inclusio with Rev 14:7: the creation formula (echoing the fourth commandment) is bookended by explicit mention of 'the commandments of God.' The two-part structure (commandments of God AND faith of Jesus) distinguishes divine commands from Jesus-specific content.
verses.notes: Greek parsing confirms: hoi terountes (V-PAP-NPM, present active participle nominative plural of tereo = the ones keeping) tas entolas (N-APF, accusative plural of entole = the commandments) tou theou (N-GSM) kai ten pistin (N-ASF, accusative singular of pistis = the faith) Iesou (N-GSM = of Jesus). The kai linking 'commandments of God' with 'faith of Jesus' uses two distinct accusative objects, showing these are two different categories of loyalty.

verses.heading: The Sabbatismos Argument — A Sabbath-Rest Remains
verses.kjv_text: Let us therefore fear, lest, a promise being left [us] of entering into his rest, any of you should seem to come short of it. For unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them: but the word preached did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in them that heard [it]. For we which have believed do enter into rest, as he said, As I have sworn in my wrath, if they shall enter into my rest: although the works were finished from the foundation of the world. For he spake in a certain place of the seventh [day] on this wise, And God did rest the seventh day from all his works. And in this [place] again, If they shall enter into my rest. Seeing therefore it remaineth that some must enter therein, and they to whom it was first preached entered not in because of unbelief: Again, he limiteth a certain day, saying in David, To day, after so long a time; as it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts. For if Jesus had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day. There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God. For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God [did] from his. Let us labour therefore to enter into that rest, lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief.
verses.context: The full argument flow: creation rest (v.4, Gen 2:2), Promised Land rest (vv.6-8), Davidic 'today' (v.7, Psa 95:11), and the remaining sabbatismos (v.9). The author uses katapausis (G2663) eight times but shifts to sabbatismos (G4520) in v.9 — a deliberate intensification, not stylistic variation. V.10 draws the hosper comparison: believers cease from works 'as God did from his.'
verses.notes: Heb 4:9 parsing: ara (particle, 'therefore') apoleipetai (V-PPI-3S, present passive indicative of apoleipo = remains/is left behind) sabbatismos (N-NSM, nominative singular of sabbatismos = Sabbath-keeping) to lao (N-DSM, dative singular = to the people) tou theou (N-GSM = of God). The -ismos suffix denotes the ACT of doing something (cf. baptismos). Lincoln concedes every extant use of sabbatismos denotes Sabbath observance. Heb 4:10: hosper (ADV, 'just as') comparison — the believer's rest is explicitly patterned on God's seventh-day rest.

verses.heading: Sabbath as Sign — 'That They Might Know I Am the LORD'
verses.kjv_text: Moreover also I gave them my sabbaths, to be a sign between me and them, that they might know that I [am] the LORD that sanctify them.
verses.context: God designates the Sabbath as owth (sign) in the context of covenant history. The purpose clause ('that they might know') establishes the Sabbath's function as a revelatory sign identifying YHWH as the one who sanctifies.
verses.notes: Paired with Eze 20:20 which repeats the owth designation. The Sabbath is explicitly called owth in Exo 31:13, 31:17, Eze 20:12, and Eze 20:20 — four times. No other commandment receives this designation.

verses.heading: Sabbath as Sign Between God and His People
verses.kjv_text: And hallow my sabbaths; and they shall be a sign between me and you, that ye may know that I [am] the LORD your God.
verses.context: Reaffirms the Sabbath as owth (sign) in the prophetic context. The sign function identifies the relationship between Creator and creature.
verses.notes: The language 'between me and you' echoes the covenant sign language of Exo 31:13,17. The four-fold owth designation (Exo 31:13, 31:17, Eze 20:12, 20:20) makes the Sabbath the most explicitly designated sign-commandment.

verses.heading: Beast Worship — Counterfeit of the First Commandment
verses.kjv_text: And they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast, saying, Who [is] like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him? And there was given unto him a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies; and power was given unto him to continue forty [and] two months. And he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven. And it was given unto him to make war with the saints, and to overcome them: and power was given him over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations. And all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world.
verses.context: The beast demands exclusive worship (counterfeiting the first commandment) and blasphemes God's name (counterfeiting the third commandment). Proskyneo (G4352) dominates: the same verb used in Rev 14:7 for worshipping the Creator.
verses.notes: Rev 13:4 uses proskyneo twice for beast-worship. Rev 14:7 uses proskyneo for Creator-worship. The worship binary is explicit: worship the beast (Rev 13) or worship the Creator (Rev 14:7).

verses.heading: Image of the Beast — Counterfeit of the Second Commandment
verses.kjv_text: And deceiveth them that dwell on the earth by [the means of] those miracles which he had power to do in the sight of the beast; saying to them that dwell on the earth, that they should make an image to the beast, which had the wound by a sword, and did live. And he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image of the beast should both speak, and cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed.
verses.context: The land beast sets up an image (eikon, G1504) to be worshipped, counterfeiting the second commandment's prohibition against graven images. The death penalty for refusal echoes Daniel 3.
verses.notes: The image-worship pattern mirrors Daniel 3's manufactured image + universal worship command + death penalty. Rev 13:15 uses eikon (G1504) for the beast's image, the same word used in the second commandment prohibition.

verses.heading: Mark of the Beast — Counterfeit Seal Against the Fourth Commandment
verses.kjv_text: And he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads: And that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark, or the name of the beast, or the number of his name.
verses.context: The mark (charagma, G5480) functions as a counterfeit of the seal of God (Rev 7:3). If the Sabbath is God's sign/seal (Exo 31:13,17; Eze 20:12,20), the mark represents a counterfeit worship identity.
verses.notes: The seal/mark binary: Rev 7:3 (seal of God on foreheads) vs. Rev 13:16-17 (mark of the beast on hand/forehead). The Sabbath-as-sign tradition (Exo 31, Eze 20) informs this contrast if the first angel's creation formula points to the fourth commandment.

verses.heading: Third Angel's Warning Against Beast Worship
verses.kjv_text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive [his] mark in his forehead, or in his hand, The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb: And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name.
verses.context: The consequences of wrong worship. The three-part description (beast + image + mark) inverts the first angel's three-part command (fear God + give glory + worship Creator). 'No rest day nor night' contrasts with the Sabbath rest.
verses.notes: 'No rest' (ouk echousin anapausin) in 14:11 for beast-worshippers contrasts with the sabbatismos (rest) remaining for God's people (Heb 4:9). Anapausis (G372) is used, a rest-family word.

verses.heading: New Heavens and New Earth — Sabbath in the New Creation
verses.kjv_text: For as the new heavens and the new earth, which I will make, shall remain before me, saith the LORD, so shall your seed and your name remain. And it shall come to pass, [that] from one new moon to another, and from one sabbath to another, shall all flesh come to worship before me, saith the LORD.
verses.context: Prophetic vision of worship in the new creation: from sabbath to sabbath, all flesh shall worship before YHWH. Creates the creation-to-new-creation bookend with Gen 2:2-3. However, the passage also mentions New Moons and v.24 (dead bodies) complicates a fully literal reading.
verses.notes: The Sabbath appears at creation (Gen 2:2-3), at Sinai (Exo 20:8-11), and in the new creation (Isa 66:22-23) — a bookend structure suggesting permanence. Paulien cautions against over-reading this passage: the New Moon problem and Isa 66:24 (gazing at dead bodies) complicate a purely literal interpretation. The study should note this honestly.

verses.heading: Ktizo in Revelation — Verb Distinction with Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: And sware by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven, and the things that therein are, and the earth, and the things that therein are, and the sea, and the things which are therein, that there should be time no longer:
verses.context: The mighty angel swears by the Creator. Uses ktizo (G2936) — 'who created (ektisen) heaven.' Rev 14:7 uses poieo (poiesanti) for the same concept. Both describe God as Creator of heaven, earth, and sea. The verb switch within the same book is significant.
verses.notes: Greek parsing confirms: ektisen (V-AAI-3S, aorist active indicative of ktizo G2936). Compare Rev 14:7 poiesanti (V-AAP-DSM, aorist active participle of poieo G4160). Rev 10:6 uses ktizo; Rev 14:7 deliberately switches to poieo. Gen 1-2 LXX and Exo 20:11 LXX use epoiesen (poieo), not ektisen. Mueller: the poieo/ktizo distinction shows Rev 14:7 deliberately echoes Exo 20:11 LXX.

verses.heading: Fountains of the Great Deep — Possible Source for 'Pegas Hydaton'
verses.kjv_text: In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month, the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened.
verses.context: The Flood narrative mentions 'fountains of the great deep.' If the LXX uses pege/pegas for 'fountains,' Rev 14:7's 'pegas hydaton' may layer a Flood judgment allusion onto the creation allusion. Baldwin identifies this as the key departure from the standard creation formula.
verses.notes: The Hebrew uses ma'yanoth (from ma'yan, H4599 — the primary Hebrew equivalent of pege G4077 per LXX mapping with PMI 8.15). Baldwin argues Rev 14:7 substitutes 'pegas hydaton' for 'panta ta en autois' to add a Flood judgment allusion alongside the creation allusion.

verses.heading: The Structural Template — Fear God + Commandments + Judgment
verses.kjv_text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this [is] the whole [duty] of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether [it be] good, or whether [it be] evil.
verses.context: Solomon's conclusion matches the first angel's message precisely: fear God (Ecc 12:13a = Rev 14:7a), keep commandments (Ecc 12:13b = Rev 14:12), judgment (Ecc 12:14 = Rev 14:7b). Four OT farewell addresses give the same triad; the first angel's message is the fifth.
verses.notes: The structural parallel is threefold: (1) fear God — yare in Ecc 12:13, phobeo in Rev 14:7; (2) keep commandments — mitsvah in Ecc 12:13, entole in Rev 14:12; (3) judgment — mishpat in Ecc 12:14, krisis in Rev 14:7. This pattern confirms the first angel's message is not a novel formulation but follows a recognizable OT template.

verses.heading: Entole Applied to the Sabbath Commandment
verses.kjv_text: And they returned, and prepared spices and ointments; and rested the sabbath day according to the commandment.
verses.context: The women 'rested the sabbath day according to the commandment (kata ten entolen).' This directly applies entole (G1785) to the Sabbath commandment, collapsing Tinker's claim that John uses entole only for Jesus's teachings and nomos for the Mosaic Law.
verses.notes: Greek parsing confirms: sabbaton (N-ASN, G4521 = the Sabbath) hesychasan (V-AAI-3P = they rested) kata (PREP = according to) ten entolen (N-ASF, G1785 = the commandment). This is the single most decisive verse against the nomos/entole distinction: entole refers directly to the Sabbath commandment.

verses.heading: Sabbath Days as Shadow — The Objection Text
verses.kjv_text: Let no man therefore judge you in meat, or in drink, or in respect of an holyday, or of the new moon, or of the sabbath [days]: Which are a shadow of things to come; but the body [is] of Christ.
verses.context: The primary objection text against ongoing Sabbath obligation. 'Sabbath days' (sabbaton, G4521) appears in a triad with 'holyday' (heorte, G1859) and 'new moon' (neomenia, G3561) — the annual/monthly/weekly ceremonial calendar triad from 1 Chr 23:31 and 2 Chr 31:3. These are called 'a shadow (skia, G4639) of things to come.'
verses.notes: Greek parsing of Col 2:16 confirms sabbaton (N-GPN, genitive plural) in the triad heortes (G1859) — neomenias (G3561) — sabbaton (G4521). Col 2:17 confirms skia (N-NSF, G4639 = shadow) ton mellonton (V-PAP-GPN = of things to come). The prior sabbath-shadow-or-memorial study established five criteria the weekly Sabbath fails for being a biblical shadow: it points backward (zakar), is the original, remains after the cross (Heb 4:9), belongs to the Decalogue, and is called 'perpetual covenant.' Lev 23:37-38 separates ceremonial sabbaths from 'the sabbaths of the LORD' using millibad (H905).

verses.heading: No Need for Sun/Moon — The Greek Is Decisive
verses.kjv_text: And the city had no need of the sun, neither of the moon, to shine in it: for the glory of God did lighten it, and the Lamb [is] the light thereof.
verses.context: The Answering Adventism objection claims Rev 21:23 says there IS no sun or moon, making Isa 66:23's New Moon worship impossible. But the Greek text says the city 'has no NEED (ou chreian echei) of the sun... to shine in it (hina phainosin aute).' The text says no need for their light function, not that they cease to exist.
verses.notes: Greek parsing is decisive: he polis ou chreian echei (the city has no need) tou heliou (of the sun) oude (nor) tes selenes (of the moon) hina phainosin aute (in order that they might shine in it). The construction is: no need + genitive of thing not needed + hina purpose clause (to shine). The text does NOT say the sun and moon cease to exist — it says the city does not need them as light sources because God's glory illuminates it. This leaves open the possibility of New Moon and Sabbath observance in the new earth.

verses.heading: Hebrews Quotes Gen 2:2 — God Rested the Seventh Day
verses.kjv_text: For he spake in a certain place of the seventh [day] on this wise, And God did rest the seventh day from all his works.
verses.context: The Hebrews author quotes Gen 2:2, grounding the rest argument in the creation Sabbath. The seventh day is explicitly named as the archetype for the rest that 'remains.'
verses.notes: Heb 4:4 provides the creation-rest foundation for the argument that culminates in sabbatismos (v.9). The seventh day is the pattern; the remaining rest is patterned on it.

verses.heading: Sabbatismos — A Sabbath-Keeping Remains for the People of God
verses.kjv_text: There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God.
verses.context: The NT hapax sabbatismos (G4520). The author shifts from katapausis (used 8x in context) to sabbatismos at the climactic verse. Every extant use of sabbatismos in Greek literature denotes Sabbath observance (Lincoln's concession). The -ismos suffix marks the act of Sabbath-keeping.
verses.notes: Parsing: ara (particle = therefore) apoleipetai (V-PPI-3S = remains) sabbatismos (N-NSM = Sabbath-keeping) to lao tou theou (to the people of God). Lincoln (anti-Sabbatarian): Plutarch, Justin, Epiphanius, Apostolic Constitutions — every extant use denotes Sabbath observance. Lane (WBC): translates 'Sabbath celebration.' The GCI counter-argument ('no point persuading Jews to do what they already did') assumes the audience was keeping the Sabbath; but the Hebrews audience may have been tempted to abandon it.

verses.heading: The Hosper Comparison — As God Did From His Works
verses.kjv_text: For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God [did] from his.
verses.context: The explicit parallel: the believer's rest is patterned on God's seventh-day rest. The hosper (G5618, 'just as') comparison draws a direct analogy between human sabbatismos and divine Sabbath rest at creation.
verses.notes: Parsing: ho eiselthon (the one having entered) eis ten katapausin autou (into his rest) kai autos katepausen (he also ceased, V-AAI-3S of katapauo G2664) apo ton ergon autou (from his works) hosper (ADV = just as) apo ton idion (from his own) ho theos (God). The hosper draws an exact parallel: as God ceased from works, so the believer ceases.

verses.heading: Ktizo for Creation in Revelation — Generic Creation Praise
verses.kjv_text: Thou art worthy, O Lord, to receive glory and honour and power: for thou hast created all things, and for thy pleasure they are and were created.
verses.context: The heavenly beings praise God as Creator using ktizo (G2936), not poieo. This is generic creation theology. Rev 14:7's switch to poieo distinguishes it from this verse and aligns it with the specific Exo 20:11 LXX vocabulary.
verses.notes: Rev 4:11 uses ektisas (aorist active of ktizo) and ektisthesan (aorist passive of ktizo). Rev 14:7 uses poiesanti (aorist active participle of poieo). The verb distinction within the same book shows deliberate vocabulary selection.

verses.heading: The Millibad Separator — Weekly Sabbath Distinguished from Ceremonial
verses.kjv_text: These [are] the feasts of the LORD, which ye shall proclaim [to be] holy convocations, to offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD, a burnt offering, and a meat offering, a sacrifice, and drink offerings, every thing upon his day: Beside the sabbaths of the LORD, and beside your gifts, and beside all your vows, and beside all your freewill offerings, which ye give unto the LORD.
verses.context: After listing the ceremonial feasts/sabbaths in Lev 23:1-36, v.38 explicitly separates them from 'the sabbaths of the LORD' using the fourfold millibad (H905, 'besides/apart from'). The ceremonial calendar is 'beside' (not including) the weekly Sabbath.
verses.notes: The fourfold millibad in v.38 creates an explicit textual separation: these feasts are 'beside' (1) the sabbaths of the LORD, (2) your gifts, (3) your vows, (4) your freewill offerings. This is the OT's own distinction between ceremonial and weekly sabbaths.

verses.heading: Vindication Worship — Who Shall Not Fear and Glorify?
verses.kjv_text: And they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying, Great and marvellous [are] thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true [are] thy ways, thou King of saints. Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for [thou] only [art] holy: for all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest.
verses.context: Post-judgment worship echoing the first angel's message: fear (phobeo), glorify (doxazo), worship (proskyneo), and judgment (krima). The song of Moses connects to the Exodus deliverance pattern.
verses.notes: The vindication declaration progression: Rev 14:7 (worship call) → Rev 15:3-4 (vindication worship) → Rev 16:7 → Rev 19:1-2. All four elements of the first angel's message recur.

verses.heading: Seal of God — Contrast with Mark of the Beast
verses.kjv_text: Saying, Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, nor the trees, till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads.
verses.context: The seal of God placed on foreheads, contrasting with the mark of the beast (Rev 13:16). If the Sabbath is the sign/seal of God's people (Exo 31:13,17; Eze 20:12,20), then the seal/mark binary maps onto the worship binary of Rev 13-14.
verses.notes: The seal (sphragis/sphragizo) is placed on foreheads — the same location as the beast's mark. The fourth commandment contains the three elements of a royal seal: name (YHWH), title (Creator), territory (heaven and earth).

verses.heading: Beast Blasphemes God's Name — Counterfeit of Third Commandment
verses.kjv_text: And he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven.
verses.context: The beast blasphemes (blasphemia, G988) God's name, counterfeiting the third commandment's prohibition against taking God's name in vain. Part of the systematic First Table engagement in Rev 13.
verses.notes: Paulien's structural argument: Rev 13:4,8 (first commandment counterfeit), Rev 13:15 (second), Rev 13:6 (third), Rev 13:16-17 (fourth). The first angel's creation formula (Rev 14:7) is the specific antidote to this fourfold counterfeit.

verses.heading: Keeping the Commandments of God — Same Root as Rev 14:12
verses.kjv_text: Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.
verses.context: Paul dismisses circumcision while affirming 'the keeping (teresis) of the commandments (entolon) of God (theou).' The phrase teresis entolon theou shares the same root as Rev 14:12's terountes tas entolas tou theou.
verses.notes: The tereo/entole/theos triad in 1 Cor 7:19 parallels Rev 14:12 exactly. Paul separates ceremonial markers (circumcision) from moral commands (commandments of God), maintaining the same distinction the study argues for.

verses.heading: Creation Theology — Worshipping Creature Rather Than Creator
verses.kjv_text: For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, [even] his eternal power and Godhead; so that they are without excuse: ... Who changed the truth of God into a lie, and worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator, who is blessed for ever. Amen.
verses.context: Romans 1 presents the inverse of Rev 14:7: humanity knew God through creation but rejected Him, choosing to worship the creature rather than the Creator. Rev 14:7 reverses this chain by commanding worship of the Creator.
verses.notes: Rom 1:25 uses ktisanta (aorist active participle of ktizo G2936) for 'Creator' — note the verb is ktizo, not poieo. Rev 14:7 deliberately uses poieo, aligning with Exo 20:11 rather than the more generic ktizo.

verses.heading: The Sabbath Made for Man — Universal Scope
verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, The sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath: Therefore the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.
verses.context: Jesus declares the Sabbath was 'made for man' (anthropos, not 'for Israel') — establishing universal scope. He claims lordship over it as Son of man.
verses.notes: The Sabbath was made (egeneto, from ginomai G1096) for man (dia ton anthropon) — for humanity generically, not for Israel specifically. This supports the creation institution argument: the Sabbath predates Israel.

verses.heading: Final Revelation Commandment Reference
verses.kjv_text: Blessed [are] they that do his commandments, that they may have right to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city.
verses.context: The final mention of commandments in Revelation. Connects keeping commandments with access to the tree of life in the new creation — a creation-to-new-creation arc.
verses.notes: The commandment thread in Revelation: 11:19 (ark) → 12:17 (keep commandments) → 14:12 (keep commandments) → 22:14 (do commandments). All use entole (G1785).

verses.heading: The Creation Rationale — Sabbath Commandment Foundation Verse
verses.kjv_text: For [in] six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them [is], and rested the seventh day: wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it.
verses.context: The creation rationale verse within the fourth commandment. Uses asah (H6213) for God's creative act, rendered as poieo in the LXX. The specific verse Rev 14:7 echoes.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Focus area 1: The verbal overlap between Rev 14:7 and Exo 20:11 through the LXX.

verses.heading: Worship the LORD Our Maker
verses.kjv_text: O come, let us worship and bow down: let us kneel before the LORD our maker. For he [is] our God; and we [are] the people of his pasture, and the sheep of his hand. To day if ye will hear his voice,
verses.context: A call to worship God as Creator/Maker. The Psalm 95 passage quoted extensively in Heb 3:7-11 to establish the rest argument culminating in sabbatismos.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation-worship connection and source text for Hebrews rest argument.

verses.heading: Creation by the Word — Heavens, Earth, Seas
verses.kjv_text: By the word of the LORD were the heavens made; and all the host of them by the breath of his mouth. He gathereth the waters of the sea together as an heap: he layeth up the depth in storehouses. Let all the earth fear the LORD: let all the inhabitants of the world stand in awe of him. For he spake, and it was [done]; he commanded, and it stood fast.
verses.context: Creation by divine word. Includes the creation triad (heavens, sea, earth) and calls for fear of the LORD as Creator.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation-worship-fear triad echoes the first angel's message structure.

verses.heading: The LORD Made the Heavens — Idols Contrasted
verses.kjv_text: For all the gods of the nations [are] idols: but the LORD made the heavens. Honour and majesty [are] before him: strength and beauty [are] in his sanctuary.
verses.context: Contrasts the Creator who made the heavens with worthless idols. The creation-worship-idolatry triad relevant to Rev 13-14.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creator vs. idols contrast echoes the Rev 13-14 worship binary.

verses.heading: He That Hath Made Us
verses.kjv_text: Know ye that the LORD he [is] God: [it is] he [that] hath made us, and not we ourselves; [we are] his people, and the sheep of his pasture.
verses.context: Creator-creature relationship established through God's making act. Uses asah for divine creative work.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creator-creature worship relationship.

verses.heading: Blessed of the LORD Which Made Heaven and Earth
verses.kjv_text: Ye [are] blessed of the LORD which made heaven and earth.
verses.context: Blessing formula identifying God as the one who made heaven and earth.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation formula in blessing context.

verses.heading: Help from the LORD Who Made Heaven and Earth
verses.kjv_text: My help [cometh] from the LORD, which made heaven and earth.
verses.context: Pilgrim psalm identifying the LORD by His creative act — the same formula as Exo 20:11.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation formula as divine identification.

verses.heading: Our Help in the Name of the LORD Who Made Heaven and Earth
verses.kjv_text: Our help [is] in the name of the LORD, who made heaven and earth.
verses.context: Another instance of the creation formula as divine identification.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation formula as divine identification.

verses.heading: The LORD That Made Heaven and Earth Bless Thee
verses.kjv_text: The LORD that made heaven and earth bless thee out of Zion.
verses.context: Priestly blessing using the creation formula.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation formula in priestly blessing.

verses.heading: The Everlasting God, Creator of the Ends of the Earth
verses.kjv_text: Hast thou not known? hast thou not heard, [that] the everlasting God, the LORD, the Creator of the ends of the earth, fainteth not, neither is weary? [there is] no searching of his understanding.
verses.context: Isaiah identifies God as the everlasting Creator who does not grow weary. Uses bara for creative act.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creator theology in Isaiah.

verses.heading: God Created the Heavens and Spread Forth the Earth
verses.kjv_text: Thus saith God the LORD, he that created the heavens, and stretched them out; he that spread forth the earth, and that which cometh out of it; he that giveth breath unto the people upon it, and spirit to them that walk therein:
verses.context: Creation as the basis for God's authority to speak. Uses bara for heavens.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation grounds divine authority.

verses.heading: God Formed the Earth to Be Inhabited
verses.kjv_text: For thus saith the LORD that created the heavens; God himself that formed the earth and made it; he hath established it, he created it not in vain, he formed it to be inhabited: I [am] the LORD; and [there is] none else.
verses.context: Uses all three creation verbs: bara (create), yatsar (form), and asah (make). Purpose of creation: habitation.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Three creation verbs in one verse; purpose of creation.

verses.heading: He Made the Earth by His Power
verses.kjv_text: He hath made the earth by his power, he hath established the world by his wisdom, and hath stretched out the heavens by his discretion.
verses.context: Creation by power, wisdom, and discretion. Uses asah for making the earth.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Creation theology with asah.

verses.heading: Pre-Sinai Sabbath — Manna Narrative
verses.kjv_text: Then said the LORD unto Moses, Behold, I will rain bread from heaven for you; and the people shall go out and gather a certain rate every day, that I may prove them, whether they will walk in my law, or no. And it shall come to pass, that on the sixth day they shall prepare [that] which they bring in; and it shall be twice as much as they gather daily. ... And he said unto them, This [is that] which the LORD hath said, To morrow [is] the rest of the holy sabbath unto the LORD ... And the LORD said unto Moses, How long refuse ye to keep my commandments and my laws? See, for that the LORD hath given you the sabbath, therefore he giveth you on the sixth day the bread of two days ... So the people rested on the seventh day.
verses.context: The Sabbath is operative BEFORE Sinai. God tests Israel's obedience regarding the Sabbath through the manna miracle. The Sabbath is called 'the rest of the holy sabbath unto the LORD' before the Ten Commandments are given.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Pre-Sinai Sabbath observance establishes the institution as predating the ceremonial law.

verses.heading: New Heaven and New Earth — Creation Arc Endpoint
verses.kjv_text: And I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away; and there was no more sea.
verses.context: The new creation. The first heaven and earth pass away; a new heaven and earth appear. 'No more sea' — the chaotic element is absent.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Endpoint of the creation-to-new-creation arc. The absence of sea (thalassa) contrasts with the creation formula's inclusion of sea.

verses.heading: A Psalm for the Sabbath Day — Creation-Themed Worship
verses.kjv_text: A Psalm [or] Song for the sabbath day. [It is a] good [thing] to give thanks unto the LORD, and to sing praises unto thy name, O most High: To shew forth thy lovingkindness in the morning, and thy faithfulness every night, Upon an instrument of ten strings, and upon the psaltery; upon the harp with a solemn sound. For thou, LORD, hast made me glad through thy work: I will triumph in the works of thy hands. O LORD, how great are thy works! [and] thy thoughts are very deep.
verses.context: The only psalm explicitly designated for the Sabbath day. Its content celebrates God's works (creation) — connecting Sabbath worship to creation themes.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The Sabbath psalm celebrates God's creative works, reinforcing the creation-worship-Sabbath connection.

verses.heading: Sabbath-Keeping Blessing Extended to Foreigners
verses.kjv_text: Blessed [is] the man [that] doeth this, and the son of man [that] layeth hold on it; that keepeth the sabbath from polluting it, and keepeth his hand from doing any evil. Neither let the son of the stranger, that hath joined himself to the LORD, speak, saying, The LORD hath utterly separated me from his people: neither let the eunuch say, Behold, I [am] a dry tree. For thus saith the LORD unto the eunuchs that keep my sabbaths, and choose [the things] that please me, and take hold of my covenant; Even unto them will I give in mine house and within my walls a place and a name better than of sons and of daughters: I will give them an everlasting name, that shall not be cut off. Also the sons of the stranger, that join themselves to the LORD, to serve him, and to love the name of the LORD, to be his servants, every one that keepeth the sabbath from polluting it, and taketh hold of my covenant; Even them will I bring to my holy mountain, and make them joyful in my house of prayer: their burnt offerings and their sacrifices [shall be] accepted upon mine altar; for mine house shall be called an house of prayer for all people.
verses.context: Sabbath-keeping extended to foreigners and eunuchs — universalizing the institution beyond Israel.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Universal scope of the Sabbath, supporting Mark 2:27 ('made for man').

verses.heading: The Sabbath as Delight — Proper Observance
verses.kjv_text: If thou turn away thy foot from the sabbath, [from] doing thy pleasure on my holy day; and call the sabbath a delight, the holy of the LORD, honourable; and shalt honour him, not doing thine own ways, nor finding thine own pleasure, nor speaking [thine own] words: Then shalt thou delight thyself in the LORD; and I will cause thee to ride upon the high places of the earth, and feed thee with the heritage of Jacob thy father: for the mouth of the LORD hath spoken [it].
verses.context: The Sabbath called 'a delight' and 'the holy of the LORD.' Proper Sabbath observance brings blessing.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Sabbath as delight, not burden.

verses.heading: Sabbath-Keeping as Covenant Faithfulness Test
verses.kjv_text: Thus saith the LORD; Take heed to yourselves, and bear no burden on the sabbath day, nor bring [it] in by the gates of Jerusalem; Neither carry forth a burden out of your houses on the sabbath day, neither do ye any work, but hallow ye the sabbath day, as I commanded your fathers. But they obeyed not, neither inclined their ear, but made their neck stiff, that they might not hear, nor receive instruction. And it shall come to pass, if ye diligently hearken unto me, saith the LORD, to bring in no burden through the gates of this city on the sabbath day, but hallow the sabbath day, to do no work therein; Then shall there enter into the gates of this city kings and princes sitting upon the throne of David ... But if ye will not hearken unto me to hallow the sabbath day ... then will I kindle a fire in the gates thereof, and it shall devour the palaces of Jerusalem, and it shall not be quenched.
verses.context: Jeremiah presents Sabbath-keeping as a test of covenant faithfulness with consequences: obedience brings blessing, disobedience brings destruction.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Sabbath as covenant faithfulness test.

verses.heading: Jesus Lord of the Sabbath
verses.kjv_text: At that time Jesus went on the sabbath day through the corn; and his disciples were an hungred, and began to pluck the ears of corn, and to eat. But when the Pharisees saw [it], they said unto him, Behold, thy disciples do that which is not lawful to do upon the sabbath day. But he said unto them, Have ye not read what David did, when he was an hungred, and they that were with him; How he entered into the house of God, and did eat the shewbread, which was not lawful for him to eat, neither for them which were with him, but only for the priests? Or have ye not read in the law, how that on the sabbath days the priests in the temple profane the sabbath, and are blameless? But I say unto you, That in this place is [one] greater than the temple. But if ye had known what [this] meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice, ye would not have condemned the guiltless. For the Son of man is Lord even of the sabbath day.
verses.context: Jesus claims lordship over the Sabbath. He corrects Pharisaic misapplication without abolishing the institution.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Jesus as Lord of the Sabbath — authority over its proper observance.

verses.heading: Psalm 95 Quotation — Entering God's Rest
verses.kjv_text: Wherefore (as the Holy Ghost saith, To day if ye will hear his voice, Harden not your hearts, as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness: When your fathers tempted me, proved me, and saw my works forty years. Wherefore I was grieved with that generation, and said, They do alway err in [their] heart; and they have not known my ways. So I sware in my wrath, They shall not enter into my rest.)
verses.context: The Psalm 95 quotation establishing the rest argument that culminates in sabbatismos (Heb 4:9). The wilderness generation forfeited rest through unbelief.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Foundation for the Heb 4:9 sabbatismos argument.

verses.heading: The Lord's Day
verses.kjv_text: I was in the Spirit on the Lord's day, and heard behind me a great voice, as of a trumpet,
verses.context: John received the Revelation on 'the Lord's day.' The identity of this day is debated.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The day John received the Revelation.

verses.heading: New Heavens and New Earth Prophecy
verses.kjv_text: For, behold, I create new heavens and a new earth: and the former shall not be remembered, nor come into mind.
verses.context: Isaiah's new creation prophecy using bara (create). Part of the creation-to-new-creation arc.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. New creation prophecy preceding the Isa 66:22-23 Sabbath passage.

verses.heading: New Heavens and New Earth Wherein Dwelleth Righteousness
verses.kjv_text: Nevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.
verses.context: Peter's expectation of the new creation, based on Isaiah's promise.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. NT expectation of new creation.

verses.heading: Christ as Creator — All Things Made by Him
verses.kjv_text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same was in the beginning with God. All things were made by him; and without him was not any thing made that was made.
verses.context: Christ as the agent of creation. Uses ginomai (G1096) for 'made,' not poieo or ktizo.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Christ as Creator — relevant to the worship call in Rev 14:7.

verses.heading: Christ as Creator — Quoting Psalm 102
verses.kjv_text: And, Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth; and the heavens are the works of thine hands: They shall perish; but thou remainest; and they all shall wax old as doth a garment; And as a vesture shalt thou fold them up, and they shall be changed: but thou art the same, and thy years shall not fail.
verses.context: Hebrews applies Psalm 102:25-27 to Christ, identifying Him as the Creator who laid the foundation of the earth.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Christ as Creator in Hebrews.

verses.heading: By Him Were All Things Created
verses.kjv_text: For by him were all things created, that are in heaven, and that are in earth, visible and invisible, whether [they be] thrones, or dominions, or principalities, or powers: all things were created by him, and for him: And he is before all things, and by him all things consist.
verses.context: Christ as the agent and purpose of all creation. Uses ktizo (G2936) for 'created.' The comprehensive scope ('all things') establishes Christ's authority as Creator.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Christ as Creator using ktizo — contrast with Rev 14:7's poieo.

word_studies.summary: The broadest Greek verb for 'do/make,' functioning as the default action verb. In creation contexts, poieo is the LXX's standard rendering of Hebrew asah (H6213), with 2,022 co-occurrences and PMI score 3.15 — the highest-confidence Hebrew-to-Greek lexical alignment. Rev 14:7 uses poiesanti (aorist active participle, dative) to identify the Creator — the same verb root as Exo 20:11 LXX (epoiese). Rev 10:6 uses ktizo instead, showing the author had both verbs available and deliberately chose poieo for Rev 14:7.
word_studies.relevance: The key verb linking Rev 14:7 to Exo 20:11 LXX. The poieo/asah bridge (2,022 LXX co-occurrences) is the linguistic mechanism by which the Hebrew Sabbath commandment's creation rationale (asah) enters the Greek NT (poieo). The deliberate choice of poieo over ktizo in Rev 14:7 distinguishes this verse from generic creation theology (Rev 4:11, Rev 10:6) and aligns it specifically with the Exo 20:11 LXX tradition.

word_studies.summary: The most semantically generic Hebrew verb of action. In creation contexts, asah denotes God's making/fashioning work, distinct from bara (create ex nihilo). Exo 20:11: 'For in six days the LORD made (asah) heaven and earth, the sea.' The LXX renders asah as poieo in 2,022 of its occurrences, establishing the bridge to Rev 14:7's poiesanti.
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew verb behind Exo 20:11's creation rationale. When the LXX translators rendered asah as poieo, they established the lexical link that Rev 14:7 picks up. The asah/poieo bridge is the strongest Hebrew-to-Greek pairing in the database (PMI 3.15).

word_studies.summary: Specialized creation verb meaning 'to create/found originally.' Used 12 times in NT, overwhelmingly in Pauline epistles. In Revelation: Rev 4:11 (ektisas/ektisthesan) and Rev 10:6 (ektisen) use ktizo; Rev 14:7 switches to poieo. The LXX bridges ktizo primarily with Hebrew bara (H1254, 16 co-occurrences, PMI 8.25) — the divine creation verb.
word_studies.relevance: The contrast verb. Rev 10:6 uses ktizo for creation; Rev 14:7 uses poieo. Since ktizo maps to bara and poieo maps to asah, the verb switch shows Rev 14:7 deliberately echoes the Exo 20:11 tradition (asah/poieo) rather than the Gen 1:1 tradition (bara/ktizo).

word_studies.summary: Hebrew creation verb meaning 'create' — exclusively divine subject. Distinguished from asah (make) and yatsar (form). The LXX renders bara primarily as ktizo (G2936). Gen 1:1 uses bara; Gen 1:7,16,26 use asah. The verb distinction in Hebrew (bara vs. asah) maps onto the Greek distinction (ktizo vs. poieo).
word_studies.relevance: Supports the poieo/ktizo argument by showing that the Hebrew verb distinction (bara/asah) maps consistently onto the Greek distinction (ktizo/poieo). Rev 14:7's poieo = asah = Exo 20:11 tradition.

word_studies.summary: The dominant worship verb in the NT, meaning to prostrate in homage. Revelation accounts for 41% of all occurrences (22 of 54 verses), making it the book's central worship term. The verb dominates the Rev 13-14 worship conflict: beast-worship (13:4,8,12,15) vs. Creator-worship (14:7). The proskynēsate in Rev 14:7 is an aorist imperative — a decisive command to worship.
word_studies.relevance: The worship verb that frames the entire Rev 13-14 conflict. The same word (proskyneo) is used for both beast-worship and Creator-worship, creating a binary: whom will you worship? The aorist imperative in Rev 14:7 demands a decisive choice.

word_studies.summary: The primary OT worship verb meaning 'to bow down, prostrate oneself.' The LXX renders shachah as proskyneo, establishing the linguistic chain from OT worship to the NT worship conflict in Revelation.
word_studies.relevance: OT background for proskyneo in Rev 14:7. The shachah → proskyneo chain connects OT worship commands to the first angel's worship imperative.

word_studies.summary: NT hapax legomenon in Heb 4:9. A verbal noun from sabbatizo (to keep the Sabbath), not a synonym for katapausis (cessation). The -ismos suffix denotes the ACT of Sabbath-keeping. Every extant use in Greek literature denotes Sabbath observance: Plutarch (De Superstitione 3), Justin (Dialogue 23:3), Epiphanius (Adversus Haereses 30:2:2), Apostolic Constitutions 2:36:2. Lincoln concedes this while arguing against ongoing Sabbath obligation.
word_studies.relevance: The deliberate shift from katapausis (used 8x) to sabbatismos at the argument's climax signals intentional Sabbath language. The lexical data is clear: sabbatismos = Sabbath observance in every extant Greek source. The GCI counter-argument ('no point persuading Jews') assumes the audience was keeping the Sabbath; but the epistle's audience may have been tempted to abandon it.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'rest/reposing down.' Used 8 times in Hebrews 3-4, once in Acts 7:49 (quoting Isa 66:1). The contrast term for sabbatismos: katapausis = cessation/repose; sabbatismos = Sabbath-keeping rest with institutional connotation. The KJV obscures the distinction by translating both as 'rest.'
word_studies.relevance: The contrast term that makes sabbatismos significant. The author uses katapausis eight times, then deliberately switches to sabbatismos at the climactic v.9 — not stylistic variation but deliberate intensification.

word_studies.summary: Greek Sabbath noun, the parent word of sabbatismos. Used for both the weekly Sabbath and in the Col 2:16 triad (heorte/neomenia/sabbaton). The context determines whether weekly or ceremonial sabbaths are in view.
word_studies.relevance: The Greek Sabbath word appearing in both positive (Luke 23:56 — kata ten entolen) and contested (Col 2:16 — shadow) contexts. The Col 2:16 triad (heorte + neomenia + sabbaton) matches the OT ceremonial calendar triad of 1 Chr 23:31.

word_studies.summary: Hebrew Sabbath noun, intensive form of shabath (to cease). Appears 89 times across 15 books. Concentrated in Leviticus (law), Nehemiah (enforcement), Ezekiel (prophetic rebuke), and Exodus (founding legislation). First occurrence: Exo 16:23 (pre-Sinai, in the manna narrative).
word_studies.relevance: The central Hebrew word for the institution under study. The Sabbath appears at creation (Gen 2), at Sinai (Exo 20), as sign (Exo 31, Eze 20), and in the new creation (Isa 66:23) — an arc the study traces.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'commandment, injunction.' Johannine concentration (34% of all uses). In Revelation: Rev 12:17 and 14:12 use 'entolas tou theou' (commandments of God). The prior law-28 study established that entole without qualifier = Decalogue content in 43/43 identifiable NT instances. Nomos (G3551) never appears in Revelation.
word_studies.relevance: The commandment term that bookends the three angels' messages (Rev 12:17, 14:12). Luke 23:56 applies entole directly to the Sabbath commandment, collapsing the nomos/entole distinction claimed by Tinker. Nomos never appears in Revelation — the book uses entole exclusively.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to keep, guard, observe.' In Revelation: 'hoi terountes tas entolas tou theou' (Rev 14:12) and 'ton terounton tas entolas tou theou' (Rev 12:17). The present participle indicates ongoing, continuous keeping. Same root as teresis in 1 Cor 7:19.
word_studies.relevance: The 'keeping' verb in both Rev 12:17 and 14:12. The tereo + entole + theos formula appears in Rev 12:17, Rev 14:12, and 1 Cor 7:19 — a consistent NT pattern for commandment-keeping.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'spring, fountain, well.' 5 of 12 NT occurrences are in Revelation. In Rev 14:7, 'pegas hydaton' (springs of waters) replaces the expected 'panta ta en autois' (all that is in them) from the standard creation formula. In Revelation judgment contexts (8:10, 16:4), pegas appears alongside rivers as freshwater sources subject to divine judgment. In redemptive contexts (7:17, 21:6), God/the Lamb is the pege of living water.
word_studies.relevance: The key departure word. Rev 14:7's 'pegas hydaton' substitutes for the expected 'panta ta en autois' (Psa 146:6, Acts 4:24, Acts 14:15). The Hebrew equivalent ma'yan (H4599, PMI 8.15) appears in Gen 7:11 ('fountains of the great deep'), supporting Baldwin's Flood allusion theory.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'sign, token, mark.' Used for covenant signs (rainbow Gen 9:12; circumcision Gen 17:11; Sabbath Exo 31:13,17 and Eze 20:12,20), miraculous signs (Exodus plagues), and confirmatory/prophetic signs. The Sabbath is explicitly called owth four times — the only commandment to receive this designation.
word_studies.relevance: The Sabbath is the ONLY commandment explicitly called owth (sign). Exo 31:13,17 and Eze 20:12,20 designate it four times. This supports the seal/mark binary in Revelation: if the Sabbath is God's sign, the mark of the beast is the counterfeit sign.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root meaning 'to remember, be mindful, mention.' Exo 20:8 opens the fourth commandment with zakar: 'Remember the sabbath day.' Zakar points backward — it is a memorial function, not a prophetic/shadow function. A memorial (zakar) is the opposite of a shadow (skia): shadows look forward; memorials look backward.
word_studies.relevance: The memorial function of the Sabbath. 'Remember' (zakar) in Exo 20:8 points backward to creation, not forward to fulfillment. This is the primary argument against Col 2:17 applying to the weekly Sabbath: shadows (skia) look forward; the Sabbath (zakar) looks backward.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root meaning 'to cease, rest, desist.' Parent verb of shabbath (the Sabbath). Gen 2:2 uses Qal wayyiqtol: wayyishbot (and he ceased/rested). Dominant Hiphil stem (56%) shows the word is used most often in prophetic judgment (God causing things to cease). The Qal covers creation and Sabbath-keeping contexts.
word_studies.relevance: The verbal root of the Sabbath institution. Gen 2:2 wayyishbot establishes the creation pattern: God ceased from work. Heb 4:10's hosper comparison draws on this exact pattern.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root meaning 'to be/make holy, sanctify.' First occurrence: Gen 2:3 — 'God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it.' Used in Piel (make holy, 41x), Hiphil (cause to be holy, 13x), and Qal (be holy, 7x). Gen 2:3 uses Piel wayyiqtol: wayeqaddesh (and he sanctified it).
word_studies.relevance: The sanctification verb applied to the seventh day at creation (Gen 2:3) and in the fourth commandment (Exo 20:11 'hallowed it'). Only the seventh day receives this verb at creation.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to keep, guard, watch, preserve.' Used for Sabbath-keeping in Exo 31:13-14: 'my sabbaths ye shall keep (shamar).' The guarding/protecting sense implies active preservation of the institution.
word_studies.relevance: The Sabbath-keeping verb in Exo 31:13-14 and Deut 5:12. Carries the sense of guarding something precious, not merely observing a rule.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'shadow.' In the typological sense (Col 2:17, Heb 8:5, Heb 10:1), a shadow is an outline/adumbration of the coming reality, contrasted with soma (body/substance) and eikon (image/true form). Col 2:17 calls the ceremonial calendar observances 'a shadow of things to come.'
word_studies.relevance: The key term in the Col 2:16-17 objection. Shadows (skia) look forward to fulfillment. The Sabbath uses zakar (remember) — looking backward. The sabbath-shadow-or-memorial study established five criteria the weekly Sabbath fails for being a biblical shadow.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'sea.' Part of the creation triad (heaven, earth, sea) in both Rev 14:7 and Rev 10:6. Revelation dominates with 27 of 92 occurrences. In Rev 21:1, 'there was no more sea' — the chaotic element is absent from the new creation.
word_studies.relevance: Third element in the creation formula shared between Rev 14:7 and Exo 20:11. Both use thalassa(n) in the same position. The LXX rendering of Hebrew yam (H3220).

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'heaven/sky.' 284 NT occurrences, highest density in Revelation (54 occurrences, 13.4 per 100 verses). Part of the creation formula in Rev 14:7 and Exo 20:11 LXX: ton ouranon is an exact match between the two texts.
word_studies.relevance: First element in the creation formula. 'Ton ouranon' in Rev 14:7 matches 'ton ouranon' in Exo 20:11 LXX exactly. Hebrew source is shamayim (H8064).

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'earth/land/ground.' 252 NT occurrences, highest in Revelation (82, 6.3 per 100 verses). Part of the creation formula: 'ten gen' in Rev 14:7 matches 'ten gen' in Exo 20:11 LXX exactly.
word_studies.relevance: Second element in the creation formula. 'Ten gen' in Rev 14:7 matches Exo 20:11 LXX exactly. Hebrew source is erets (H776).

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'judgment, judicial process.' Used in Rev 14:7 for 'the hour of his judgment' (he hora tes kriseos autou). The prior 3am-07 study established the krisis/katakrima distinction: krisis = evaluation, katakrima = condemnation.
word_studies.relevance: The judgment term in Rev 14:7. The creation formula is set within a judgment announcement: worship the Creator BECAUSE judgment has come. The Ecc 12:13-14 template pairs creation-worship with judgment.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to fear, be afraid, revere.' Rev 14:7 uses the aorist passive imperative phobethete — 'Fear God.' The prior 3am-05 study established the OT fear-of-God tradition (yare/phobos) as the foundation for the first angel's message.
word_studies.relevance: First imperative in the first angel's message. The fear/commandments/judgment triad matches the Ecc 12:13-14 template.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'glory, honor, splendor.' Rev 14:7: 'give glory to him' (dote auto doxan). The prior 3am-06 study established seven modes of glory-giving. Rev 21:23: 'the glory of God did lighten it.'
word_studies.relevance: Second imperative content in the first angel's message. In Rev 21:23, God's doxa replaces the need for sun/moon light, which is relevant to the Isa 66:23 question.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'law.' Critically, nomos NEVER appears in Revelation. The book uses entole exclusively for God's commands. This undermines Tinker's claim that John uses nomos for Mosaic Law and entole for Jesus's teachings.
word_studies.relevance: The absent term. Nomos never appears in Revelation, falsifying the claim that John distinguishes nomos (Mosaic Law) from entole (Jesus's teachings) in his eschatological vocabulary.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to cause to rest, to cease.' Verbal form of katapausis. Used in Heb 4:10: katepausen (aorist active indicative) = 'he ceased from his works.' The hosper comparison: as God katapauo'd from His works, so the believer ceases.
word_studies.relevance: The verbal form of katapausis, used in the hosper comparison (Heb 4:10) that patterns human rest on God's seventh-day rest.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'rest, resting place.' OT rest concept distinct from shabbath. Used for the Promised Land as resting place.
word_studies.relevance: OT background for the Heb 3-4 rest argument. The Psalm 95 quotation in Heb 3:11 uses katapausis to render the concept of God's rest/resting place.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to rest, settle down.' Gen 2:2 in some versions uses this verb (though BHSA shows shabath). The creation rest concept.
word_studies.relevance: Part of the broader rest vocabulary. Exo 20:11 uses nuwach for 'rested the seventh day' (wayyanuach), which the LXX renders with katapauo.

word_studies.summary: Masculine plural noun meaning 'God, gods.' The Creator designation in Gen 1-2. Used in Gen 2:2-3: 'God (elohiym) ended his work... God blessed the seventh day and sanctified it.'
word_studies.relevance: The Creator designation in the creation narrative and the fourth commandment. 'God (elohiym) made (asah) heaven and earth' is the formula underlying Rev 14:7.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'God.' Appears in 'entolas tou theou' (commandments of God) in Rev 12:17 and 14:12, and in 'to lao tou theou' (the people of God) in Heb 4:9.
word_studies.relevance: The genitive 'tou theou' in Rev 14:12 specifies whose commandments are being kept — God's, not merely Jesus's teachings.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'Lord, master.' Exo 20:11 LXX: 'epoiese Kyrios ton ouranon kai ten gen' (the Lord made heaven and earth). The 'LORD' (YHWH) of the fourth commandment is rendered Kyrios in the LXX.
word_studies.relevance: The LXX rendering of YHWH in the Sabbath commandment. The 'Lord of the Sabbath' claim by Jesus (Mark 2:28) uses the same title.

word_studies.summary: The covenant name of God. Appears in the fourth commandment: 'the sabbath of the LORD (YHWH) thy God' (Exo 20:10). The Sabbath contains the seal elements: name (YHWH), title (Creator who made), territory (heaven and earth).
word_studies.relevance: The fourth commandment uniquely contains all three elements of a royal seal: name (YHWH), title (who made), and territory (heaven, earth, sea). This informs the seal/mark contrast in Rev 7:3 and Rev 13:16.

word_studies.summary: Noun meaning 'earth, land.' Part of the creation formula in Exo 20:11: shamayim (heaven) + erets (earth) + yam (sea). The LXX renders erets as ge (G1093).
word_studies.relevance: Part of the Hebrew creation formula that the LXX renders into Greek, creating the verbal link to Rev 14:7.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'heavens, sky.' Part of the creation formula: 'the LORD made shamayim (heavens) and erets (earth).' The LXX renders shamayim as ouranos (G3772).
word_studies.relevance: Part of the Hebrew creation formula. The LXX renders shamayim as ouranon, matching Rev 14:7's ton ouranon exactly.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'rest, refreshment.' Used in Rev 14:11: beast-worshippers have 'no rest (anapausis) day or night.' Another rest-family term, distinct from both katapausis and sabbatismos.
word_studies.relevance: The ironic contrast: those who refuse Creator-worship (Rev 14:7) receive 'no rest' (Rev 14:11). The rest denied to beast-worshippers is the counterpoint to the sabbatismos offered to God's people (Heb 4:9).

word_studies.summary: Noun meaning 'sabbatical rest, special holiday.' Used for ceremonial rest days distinct from the weekly Sabbath. Helps distinguish weekly from ceremonial sabbaths in the Levitical calendar.
word_studies.relevance: Supports the weekly/ceremonial sabbath distinction. Shabbathown is used for ceremonial sabbaths; shabbath is used for the weekly Sabbath. This distinction matters for the Col 2:16 debate.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to serve, work, worship.' The OT worship/service verb. The Exodus pattern: 'Let my people go, that they may serve (abad) me.'
word_studies.relevance: OT worship vocabulary. The Exodus worship mandate ('serve me') provides background for Rev 14:7's worship command.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to rest, refresh.' Verbal form of anapausis. Part of the broader rest vocabulary in the NT.
word_studies.relevance: Part of the rest vocabulary family. Distinguished from katapauo and sabbatismos.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'world, ordered system.' Acts 17:24 uses kosmos ('God that made the world') while Rev 14:7 uses the specific creation formula (heaven, earth, sea). The shift from general (kosmos) to specific (creation formula) is part of the argument that Rev 14:7 deliberately echoes Exo 20:11.
word_studies.relevance: The contrast term. Generic creation texts use kosmos (Acts 17:24) or ktizo (Rev 4:11). Rev 14:7 uses the specific four-element formula with poieo, aligning with Exo 20:11 rather than generic creation language.

word_studies.summary: Adjective meaning 'holy, set apart.' The holiness of the Sabbath day: 'Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy (qadosh).' Derived from the same root as qadash (H6942).
word_studies.relevance: The holiness designation of the Sabbath. Only the seventh day is called holy at creation (Gen 2:3, via the verb qadash).

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'commandment.' Hebrew counterpart of entole. Used in Ecc 12:13: 'Fear God, and keep his commandments (mitsvoth).'
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew term behind the Ecc 12:13-14 template that matches the first angel's message structure.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to judge, decide.' The judgment verb. Col 2:16 uses krinetō ('let no one judge you') regarding sabbath days — a command not to let others judge, not an abolition of the observance itself.
word_studies.relevance: The judgment verb in Col 2:16 and Acts 17:31. The Col 2:16 context is about not being judged BY OTHERS for observing these days, not about abolishing them.

parallels.connection_type: verbal + thematic + structural — Four shared terms in sequence (poieo/asah, ouranon/shamayim, gen/erets, thalassan/yam). Same verb root (poieo = LXX rendering of asah). The fourth element diverges (pegas hydaton vs. panta ta en autois). Strongest OT allusion in Revelation per Paulien.
verbal — Both use the creation formula with poieo + ouranon + gen + thalassan. Acts 4:24 follows Exo 20:11 exactly with 'panta ta en autois.' Rev 14:7 departs with 'pegas hydaton.'
verbal — Same creation formula as Acts 4:24. Also uses 'panta ta en autois.' Confirms the standard NT creation formula.
verbal — Same creation formula. Uses 'panta ta en autois' in the LXX. Rev 14:7's departure is conspicuous against this consistent pattern.
verbal + thematic — Most expansive version of the creation formula. Uses asah + shamayim + erets + yam + all therein. Connects creation to worship explicitly.
thematic + structural — Creator God (poieo, v.24) + universal scope (v.26,30) + judgment day (v.31). Closest NT structural parallel to Rev 14:7's combination of creation, worship, and judgment.
structural — Fear God + commandments + judgment = the first angel's message template. The Ecclesiastes triad recurs in four OT farewell addresses.
vocabulary contrast — Both describe God as Creator of heaven, earth, sea. Rev 10:6 uses ktizo; Rev 14:7 uses poieo. The verb switch within the same book is deliberate.
vocabulary — 'Fountains of the great deep' (ma'yanoth, Hebrew cognate of pege). Rev 14:7's 'pegas hydaton' may layer a Flood judgment allusion onto the creation allusion.

structural inclusio — Both use tereo + entolas + tou theou. Rev 12:17 opens the cosmic conflict section; Rev 14:12 closes the three angels' messages. The creation formula of 14:7 sits within this commandment frame.
vocabulary — Same tereo/entole/theos triad. Paul separates circumcision (ceremonial) from commandments of God (moral).
thematic — Heb 4:4 quotes Gen 2:2. The sabbatismos remaining for God's people (4:9) is patterned on God's seventh-day rest at creation.
quotation — Heb 3:7-11 quotes Psa 95 extensively. The 'rest' that the wilderness generation failed to enter is the foundation for the sabbatismos argument.
thematic — Sabbath as owth (sign, Exo 31:17) parallels seal of God (sphragis, Rev 7:3). If the Sabbath is the sign/seal, the mark of the beast (Rev 13:16) is its counterfeit.

structural bookend — Sabbath at creation (Gen 2) and in the new creation (Isa 66:22-23). The bookend suggests permanence, though Isa 66:23-24 complications (New Moon, dead bodies) require honest engagement.

vocabulary contrast — Rev 4:11 uses ktizo for creation praise; Rev 14:7 uses poieo. Both are creation-worship texts in Revelation, but the verb choice distinguishes them. Rev 14:7 is specific (Exo 20:11 allusion); Rev 4:11 is generic.

vocabulary — Luke 23:56 applies entole (G1785) to the Sabbath commandment ('kata ten entolen'). Rev 14:12 uses entole for 'commandments of God.' The same word covers Sabbath-keeping and the eschatological remnant's commandment-keeping.
thematic — Vindication worship echoing the first angel: fear + glorify + worship + judgment. The same four elements recur in the post-judgment song.

structural — The heorte/neomenia/sabbaton triad in Col 2:16 matches the annual/monthly/weekly ceremonial calendar triad in 1 Chr 23:31 and 2 Chr 31:3, identifying ceremonial rather than weekly sabbaths.
thematic inversion — Rom 1:25 describes worshipping creature over Creator (using ktizo). Rev 14:7 commands worshipping the Creator (using poieo). The first angel reverses the Romans 1 chain.
